original_hypothesis.title: permit() in test token increments nonce inside structHash pre-validation; failed signatures can consume nonce and DoS future valid permits
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: GTC.sol permit() uses `nonces[owner]++` inside the struct hash encoding before signature checks. If any downstream code path could cause nonce to be incremented even when the call fails (e.g., via non-reverting flows or unexpected EVM behavior in forks), attackers could grief owners by burning nonces and invalidating pre-signed permits. While typical Solidity reverts roll back state, this pattern is still operationally risky and should be validated carefully.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a known footgun pattern. Even if safe under revert semantics, it increases audit/ops complexity and makes integration assumptions brittle. If used in production, it can cause intermittent permit failures and support incidents.
deployment_assumptions: GTC.sol is used in production or in an environment where permit-based flows matter
There exist signed permits that users rely on (meta-txs, gasless approvals)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No evidence provided that production uses this mock token; treat as deployment-dependent.

proof_of_signal.pattern: structHash includes nonces[owner]++ before require() checks

preconditions: Mock GTC token permit() is used by integrators/users
There is some path where nonce increments are not rolled back as expected (to be validated), or integrations assume a different nonce progression

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Nonce mutation occurs before signature validity checks in source expression

operational_questions: Does any failure mode allow nonce consumption (e.g., low-level calls that ignore revert, forked VM quirks)?
Do integrations rely on pre-signed permits that could be invalidated by unexpected nonce progression?
validation_requirements: Write tests that call permit with invalid sig and confirm nonce does not change post-revert
Review any meta-tx relayers or contracts that call permit via low-level call and ignore failures

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Submit permit() calls with invalid signatures for a target owner
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If nonce can be advanced, invalidate their previously prepared permits
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Exploit resulting inability to approve spenders via permit in dependent protocols

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permit-based UX degraded; potential DoS for gasless approval flows
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low to Medium (users can fall back to approve if available)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Repeated failed permit transactions targeting same owners
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Permit failures despite correct signatures (integration mismatch)

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
